title: Lamb Weston Holdings, Inc. Stockholders Should Contact Robbins LLP for Information About the Company's Failure to Disclose Significant Problems with its Enterprise Resource Planning Software System
author: Robbins Llp
description: Lamb Weston Holdings faces a securities class action lawsuit for failing to disclose significant problems with its Enterprise Resource Planning software system. The company revealed in April 2024 that the ERP rollout caused $135 million in lost sales and required a $330 million reduction in sales guidance, resulting in a $19.59 per share stock price decline. A court order in May 2026 allowed the litigation to proceed.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Company failed to disclose critical ERP system problems, resulting in $135 million in lost sales, $330 million guidance reduction, significant stock price decline, and ongoing securities litigation with court approval to proceed.

title: Be Wary of This S&P 500 Stock After Lamb Weston's Surprise Index Drop
author: Micah Zimmerman
description: Lamb Weston's recent demotion from the S&P 500 serves as a cautionary tale for Campbell's, which is following a similar declining trajectory. Campbell's missed earnings estimates, cut full-year guidance, and has seen its stock fall 40% in 12 months, putting it at risk of S&P 500 removal due to shrinking market capitalization and deteriorating business fundamentals.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Company missed Q2 earnings by 11%, cut full-year guidance significantly, stock down 40% in 12 months, operating margins deteriorating, tariff headwinds compressing margins by 230 basis points, and market cap now second-lowest in S&P 500, putting it at risk of index removal despite its historical significance.

Recently demoted from S&P 500 to S&P SmallCap 600 due to years of stock declines, soft restaurant traffic, and declining market capitalization, serving as a cautionary precedent for similar struggling consumer companies.

title: Frozen Out: Lamb Weston Beats Earnings, but the Stock Still Slides
author: Chris Markoch
description: Lamb Weston beat Q3 FY2026 earnings expectations with $1.56B revenue and $0.72 EPS, but the stock fell over 8% in 2026. Despite revenue growth, earnings declined significantly year-over-year due to margin pressure from supply dynamics and softer international demand. However, lower input costs expected in 2027 and the company's $250M cost-saving initiative could provide a catalyst for recovery, making the stock potentially attractive for value investors at levels unseen since 2017.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Mixed signals: company beat earnings expectations and grew revenue, but adjusted EPS declined significantly YoY (from $1.10 to $0.72) due to margin pressure. Stock down 8% in 2026 despite earnings beat. However, lower input costs expected in 2027, strong free cash flow ($339M YTD), and analyst price target of $51.50 (31.5% upside) suggest potential recovery, making it a neutral-to-positive contrarian opportunity for value investors.

Mentioned as bucking market trends despite challenging consumer environment, suggesting strong operational performance and demand for Lamb Weston's products, which is a positive indicator for the supplier relationship.

title: $50 Million Still at Stake as Manager Cuts Exposure to Volatile Food Stock Down 28% This Past Year
author: Jonathan Ponciano
description: Douglas Lane & Associates sold 65,461 shares of Lamb Weston Holdings in Q4, reducing its position to 1.2 million shares valued at $50.85 million. The stock has declined 28% over the past year, underperforming the S&P 500 by over 40 percentage points. Despite recent operational improvements including positive earnings swings and dividend increases, the market remains unforgiving due to pricing pressures and customer concessions.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Stock has declined 28% over the past year, significantly underperforming the S&P 500. Despite operational stabilization shown in Q2 results (1% sales growth, positive earnings swing), the market remains unforgiving due to persistent pricing pressures, customer concessions, and uneven international utilization. The fund manager's 28% reduction in exposure signals reduced conviction.
Mentioned as a top holding of Douglas Lane & Associates ($314.35 million, 4.4% of AUM) but no specific news or analysis provided about the company itself.

title: 5 Under-the-Radar Consumer Staples Stocks With Pricing Power
author: Chris Markoch
description: Consumer staples stocks underperformed in 2025, but mid-cap names with pricing power and margin protection strategies present opportunities for 2026. Five beaten-down stocks—Hormel Foods, Conagra Brands, Lamb Weston, Post Holdings, and J.M. Smucker—offer solid earnings growth projections, attractive valuations, and dividend yields, with upside potential ranging from 21% to 30%.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Despite 25% stock decline in 2025 due to tariffs and operational issues, company maintains strong top-line growth and dual pricing power through branded products (SPAM, Applegate, Skippy) and private label manufacturing. Consensus price target of $28.20 implies 28% upside, plus 60-year dividend king status with 5.12% yield.

Stock dropped 37% in 2025, but company's AI-driven brand rationalization focuses on higher-margin categories. Analysts forecast 19% upside with favorable valuation and 8.4% dividend yield, positioning it well for margin expansion through targeted pricing and mix improvements.

Despite 34% decline in 2025, company benefits from unique pricing power in frozen retail and contract-based foodservice pricing. Analysts project 17% YOY earnings growth with consensus price target of $54.18, representing 30% upside potential from January 6 closing price.

Best performer on the list with only 9.8% decline in 2025. Strong position in sticky cereal category with brand tiering strategy provides pricing flexibility. Analysts project 15% YOY earnings growth with consensus price target of $127.33, implying 27% upside.

Despite 2025 pressure from coffee exposure, company protects margins through product mix optimization and incremental pricing rather than headline hikes. Consensus price target of $116.79 implies 21% upside, plus dividend aristocrat status with 27 consecutive years of increases and 4.59% yield.

ETF was nearly flat in 2025, reflecting sector underperformance. Article suggests sector presents opportunities in 2026 as beaten-down mid-cap names with pricing power offer better risk-reward than mega-cap alternatives like Procter & Gamble and Coca-Cola.
